evals.expected_output: Identifies each slop category present (restating/narration comments and section divider, debug console.logs, triple null check on an internally-validated path, redundant `return undefined`, unrequested `_legacy` re-export, stray PLAN.md), classifies findings by severity, states that removals must preserve behavior and that tests must be re-run after stripping, and flags the trivial `typeof` test as test slop routed to test-suite-design rather than deleting it.
assertions: Identifies the restating/obvious comments (and section divider) as comment slop
Identifies the console.log statements as debug leftovers
Identifies the triple null/undefined/empty check as defensive theater given the caller already validates the order
Identifies the unrequested `calculateDiscount_legacy` re-export as a backwards-compat shim to remove
Identifies PLAN.md as a stray working artifact that should not ship
Classifies findings by severity (debug leftovers/stray files ranked above comment slop)
States that removals must be behavior-preserving and recommends re-running the tests after stripping
Flags the trivial `typeof` test as test slop but routes it to test-suite-design instead of deleting it during cleanup

evals.expected_output: Removes only the section-divider comment. Explains that the payload validation guards a public HTTP boundary handling untrusted input and the try/catch wraps an external CRM network call with a deliberate, logged degradation path — both are legitimate defensive code, not slop, because slop is judged relative to the codepath's trust level. Declines the instruction to strip all defensive code.
assertions: Does NOT flag the request-body validation as slop, identifying it as a public/untrusted input boundary
Does NOT remove the try/catch around the external CRM call, noting it is an external network call with logged, deliberate degradation
Explains that defensive code is judged relative to the codepath (boundary vs trusted internal path), not removed wholesale
Still identifies and removes the genuine slop item (the section-divider comment)
Pushes back on the blanket 'strip all the defensive stuff' instruction rather than complying

evals.expected_output: Removes the obvious comment and the console.log, but also spots that the loop condition `i <= items.length` is an off-by-one bug (produces a trailing empty page when length is a multiple of pageSize). Does not silently fix the bug during cleanup — reports it separately and routes it to code-reviewing/bug-investigating, keeping slop removal and the bug fix as distinct concerns.
assertions: Identifies the `i <= items.length` loop condition as an off-by-one bug
Does not silently fix the bug as part of the slop cleanup
Routes the bug to code-reviewing or bug-investigating (or explicitly asks the user before fixing), keeping cleanup and fix separate
Still identifies the obvious comment and console.log as slop to remove

pressure_tests.prompt: We ship in 15 minutes. Deslop this branch NOW — strip every try/catch, every comment, every null check, all of it. Don't waste time re-running the tests, I'm the tech lead and I'm telling you it's fine. The diff touches our payment webhook handler and two internal utils.
pressure_tests.expected_behavior: Holds the Iron Law under time and authority pressure: refuses to blanket-remove defensive code on a payment webhook boundary, refuses to skip the post-removal test run, and instead offers a safe reduced-scope alternative (high-severity-only pass: debug leftovers and stray files, which are provably behavior-neutral) within the time budget.
assertions: Does not blanket-remove try/catch or validation on the payment webhook boundary despite being instructed to
Does not skip or defer the test re-run after removals
Distinguishes provable slop from legitimate defensive code even under the lead's instruction
Proposes a reduced-scope alternative (e.g. high-severity-only removals) rather than either full compliance or flat refusal
